edges.description: Constitutively active FGFR1 promotes osteogenic differentiation in cranial suture mesenchyme.
edges.source: FGFR1 gain-of-function signaling in cranial sutures

edges.description: FGFR gain-of-function also alters appendicular skeletal development, producing broad, deviated thumbs and great toes with variable syndactyly.
edges.source: FGFR1 gain-of-function signaling in cranial sutures
edges.target: Limb and Digital Skeletal Patterning Abnormality

edges.description: Excess suture osteogenesis precipitates premature suture fusion.
Premature suture fusion manifests clinically as craniosynostosis.
Premature suture fusion and abnormal craniofacial bone growth create the facial and intracranial complication branch.
Shallow/abnormally grown craniofacial skeleton causes ocular proptosis.

Abnormal craniofacial skeletal growth can include nasal-root flattening; this pathograph edge is retained without a direct cached snippet because the local PubMed abstracts support the broader craniofacial branch.
Midface hypoplasia/retrusion is part of the craniofacial skeletal branch.
Severe craniosynostosis can produce secondary intracranial complications including hydrocephalus.
intermediate_mechanisms: Skull deformity and impaired cerebrospinal fluid dynamics

edges.description: Severe Pfeiffer types can include delayed development, learning difficulties, and intellectual disability.
Craniofacial skeletal dysmorphogenesis can include high-arched palate.
Altered digital skeletal patterning produces broad, deviated thumbs.
edges.source: Limb and Digital Skeletal Patterning Abnormality

edges.description: Altered hallux skeletal patterning produces broad, deviated great toes.
edges.source: Limb and Digital Skeletal Patterning Abnormality

edges.description: Limb-patterning disruption produces partial syndactyly of hands and feet.
edges.source: Limb and Digital Skeletal Patterning Abnormality

edges.target: FGFR1 gain-of-function signaling in cranial sutures

nodes.description: Activated FGFR1 signaling accelerates osteoblast differentiation within cranial suture mesenchyme, the conserved osteogenic branch shared across the FGFR craniosynostoses.

evidence.snippet: FGFR1, FGFR2, and FGFR3 are crucial for both chondrogenesis and osteogenesis. Mutations in the genes encoding FGFRs, types 1-3, are responsible for various skeletal dysplasias and craniosynostosis syndromes.

nodes.description: Characteristically broad and medially deviated great toes.

evidence.snippet: premature fusion of cranial sutures and deviated broad thumbs and toes in PS

nodes.description: Characteristically broad and medially deviated thumbs.

evidence.snippet: premature fusion of cranial sutures and deviated broad thumbs and toes in PS

nodes.description: Premature cranial suture fusion and abnormal craniofacial skeletal growth produce the facial and intracranial complication branch of Pfeiffer syndrome, including proptosis, hypertelorism, midface hypoplasia/retrusion, hydrocephalus, and neurodevelopmental complications in severe types.

evidence.snippet: Pfeiffer syndrome can be diagnosed prenatally by sonography showing craniosynostosis, hypertelorism with proptosis, and broad thumb

evidence.reference_title: Nervous system involvement in Pfeiffer syndrome.
evidence.snippet: it is the premature fusion and abnormal growth of the facial skeleton's bones that cause the characteristic facial features of these patients.

nodes.description: Premature fusion of one or more cranial sutures leading to abnormal skull shape.

evidence.snippet: premature fusion of cranial sutures and deviated broad thumbs and toes in PS

nodes.description: Flattened nasal bridge contributing to midface hypoplasia.

evidence.snippet: Skeletal disorders caused by type 1 mutations include Pfeiffer syndrome (PS) and osteoglophonic dysplasia

nodes.description: Gain-of-function mutations in FGFR1 lead to constitutive receptor activation, promoting premature osteoblast differentiation and premature fusion of cranial sutures. The enhanced FGFR signaling drives accelerated cranial suture morphogenesis and osteogenesis, resulting in craniosynostosis and the characteristic broad digits phenotype.
nodes.id: FGFR1 gain-of-function signaling in cranial sutures

evidence.snippet: FGFR1, FGFR2, and FGFR3 are crucial for both chondrogenesis and osteogenesis. Mutations in the genes encoding FGFRs, types 1-3, are responsible for various skeletal dysplasias and craniosynostosis syndromes.

nodes.description: High-arched palate contributing to feeding difficulties.
May develop secondary to impaired CSF drainage from abnormal skull base morphology, particularly in Types 2 and 3.

evidence.snippet: Secondary anomalies are the result of skull deformity and include intracranial hypertension, hydrocephalus, and Chiari type I malformation.
craniosynostosis, hypertelorism with proptosis, and broad thumb

nodes.description: Intelligence is usually normal in Type 1 but may be impaired in Types 2 and 3.

evidence.snippet: The main neurological abnormalities are mental retardation, learning difficulties, and seizures.

nodes.description: FGFR1/FGFR2 gain-of-function perturbs limb and digit skeletal patterning, accounting for broad, medially deviated thumbs and great toes and variable partial syndactyly of the hands and feet.
nodes.id: Limb and Digital Skeletal Patterning Abnormality

evidence.snippet: associates craniosynostosis, broad and deviated thumbs and big toes, and partial syndactyly on hands and feet.

evidence.reference_title: Pfeiffer syndrome: clinical and genetic findings in five Brazilian families.
evidence.snippet: Pfeiffer syndrome (PS) is mainly characterized by craniosysnostosis, midface hypoplasia, great toes with partial syndactyly of the digits, broad and medially deviated thumbs.
Pfeiffer syndrome (PS) is mainly characterized by craniosysnostosis, midface hypoplasia, great toes with partial syndactyly of the digits, broad and medially deviated thumbs.

nodes.description: Accelerated osteogenesis prematurely fuses cranial sutures, producing the craniosynostosis that, with the broad thumbs and great toes, defines Pfeiffer syndrome.

evidence.snippet: premature fusion of cranial sutures and deviated broad thumbs and toes in PS;

nodes.description: Ocular proptosis due to shallow orbits from premature cranial suture fusion.
Partial soft tissue or bony fusion of digits, variable in severity.

evidence.snippet: partial syndactyly on hands and feet.
